nativeRange.habitat: Steep outer reef slopes between roughly 7 and 100 m (typically 20–70 m), where individuals shelter in ledges, caves and crevices and rarely venture more than a few centimetres from an escape hole.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been reported to graze on encrusting growth, sponges, tunicates and algal films among reef rubble. In captivity, generally advised to offer multiple small feedings per day of varied marine fare — Spirulina, marine algae sheets, high-quality angelfish preparations (ideally including sponge-based formulas), mysis and other small meaty items. A mature system with grazable live rock tends to help reluctant specimens transition; sponge inclusion in prepared foods is commonly considered important for long-term success.

avoidWith: boisterous tangs or large angels that bully shy fish

summary: Rare, expert-only deep-reef dwarf angelfish from the Indo-Pacific, marked by alternating bold black and pale vertical bars. Shy and reclusive, notoriously finicky feeders that commonly starve in captivity without careful diet planning.
careNotes: The Multibar Angelfish is widely classified as expert-only and is rarely available in the trade. Wild specimens are typically collected from steep outer reef slopes at 20–70 m where they shelter in caves and crevices, so a mature reef system with abundant live rock, deep caves and shaded overhangs is generally advised — replicating that structural complexity helps reduce stress on shy specimens. Subdued lighting (or a deeply shaded section of the rockwork) is commonly considered helpful at first, with gradual brightening over time. Reef compatibility is rated 'with caution': isolated cases of nipping at sessile invertebrates, LPS coral polyps, soft corals and clam mantles (Tridacna especially) have been reported, though captive-bred specimens are reported to be less destructive. Tankmate selection matters — pair only with calm, similarly-sized or smaller fish that will not outcompete it at feeding time, and avoid keeping more than one Multibar (or another dwarf angel) in the system unless introduced as a carefully matched pair. Like other dwarf angelfish, individuals are generally considered protogynous hermaphrodites that form loose harems on the reef with one dominant male over several females. Feeding is the major captive hurdle: FishBase explicitly notes specimens 'usually starve when kept in captivity' and LiveAquaria flags the species as a 'finicky eater' that prefers a variety of small meaty items, angelfish/herbivore preparations and live sponges. Multiple small feedings per day, sponge-containing prepared foods, and a mature live-rock system tend to give the best results; specimens that do not begin grazing within the first week are at high risk. The species is generally considered sensitive to transport and acclimation. IUCN Red List status is Least Concern. Cross-source notes on tank size: LiveAquaria and Tropical-Fish-Keeping recommend 70 US gal (~265 L) minimum, ReefApp lists 300 L, and other vendors suggest at least 75 gal (~284 L). We set 300 L as a conservative cautious-larger figure given the species' shy temperament, deep-cave biotope, grazing requirement and reported need for ample rockwork.
breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner. Captive rearing has reportedly been achieved at a commercial scale (captive-bred specimens are occasionally available) but is not routine for hobbyists. Individuals are generally considered protogynous hermaphrodites that form harems with one dominant male over multiple females; the largest, most dominant fish in a group tends to transform into the male.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of aquarium husbandry parameters (tank size 70 gal / ~265 L, water parameters, semi-aggressive temperament, with-caution reef rating, finicky-feeder warning, omnivorous diet, expert-only care level).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes, original description Smith & Radcliffe, 1911), max size (12 cm TL — broadly agrees with LiveAquaria's 4.5 in / ~11 cm), depth range (7–100 m, usually 20–70 m), preferred temperature (25.3–29.1 °C, mean 27.5 °C), Indo-Pacific distribution (Cocos-Keeling Atoll to Society Islands, north to Yaeyama Islands, south to Great Barrier Reef), reclusive biology, and IUCN Least Concern status. FishBase flags that specimens 'usually starve when kept in captivity' — a key driver of the difficult feedingDifficulty rating.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Used as a second secondary after Reef2Reef wiki returned no match and Reefs.com returned HTTP 403. Cross-checked minimum tank size (300 L), reef-safe rating (often reef safe with caveats — clam-nipping risk), diet emphasis on Spirulina/sponges/small crustaceans, protogynous hermaphrodite reproduction, and 'fastidious / difficult to feed' care difficulty notes.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Used as a third confirming source for tank size (70 gal / ~265 L), semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe-with-caution rating (clam-mantle and coral nipping reports), water parameters (22–26 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), lifespan up to 8 years, sponge-inclusive diet requirement, and protogynous-hermaphrodite/haremic breeding biology.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner; commercial captive rearing has reportedly been achieved but is not routine at hobbyist scale. Protogynous hermaphrodite, almost certainly haremic.
